Wake-Up Words 2: “Alexa, ask airport weather to read the metar at kilo echo whiskey romeo”
Wake-Up Words 3: “Alexa, ask airport weather to get the atis at Cincinnati”
Description: This skill obtains airport weather data and reads it aloud. For observations (METARS) the format matches that of airport ATIS.  The data comes from http://www.aviationweather.gov.!!! PILOTS -- This is NOT a source for a legal weather briefing! Get the OFFICIAL weather before launching !!!The content of METARs and ATIS, though similar, are not the same. This skill translates the METAR data to be as like ATIS as possible. ICAO aviation phonetics are used and wind directions are magnetic-north referenced.  ATIS often contains airport-specific information not present in METARs. Pilots, this skill is not a substitute for real ATIS!There is no standard "out loud" format for TAFs. The skill just does its best to present the forecast clearly.There are two ways to specify the station. The first is by three or four letter ICAO airport identifier. If you only provide three letters, the skill assumes you are referencing a US airport by prepending prepends a 'K'. You can specify any airport worldwide by providing all four letters. The letters must be provided using ICAO phonetics. (goo.gl/kQKUKL)The second approach is by city name. Unfortunately, my database of city names is limited. If your city does not have a large airport, it may not be in the database. If your city name is common, you can try including the state and/or country. If your city name is unique, do NOT include the state or country. I'm experimenting with how best to disambiguate cities of the same name. I'm open to suggestions.There are some interesting additional features you can try:- Get the TAF like this: "Alexa, ask airport weather to get the forecast for kilo juliet foxtrot kilo" "Alexa, ask airport weather to get the taf for Houston"- Get the Zulu time, like this: "Alexa, ask airport weather to report zulu time"- set a preferred compass reference for METARs:"Alexa, ask airport weather to set wind reference to magnetic" or"Alexa, ask airport weather to set wind reference to true"(Real ATIS is ALWAYS magnetically referenced. TAF is always true.)- set a preferred visibility unit:"Alexa, ask airport weather to set visibility to miles""Alexa, ask airport weather to set visibility to kilometers"In kilometer mode, the visibility report will be say "kilometers" (or "meters" if less than 1 km). In miles mode, no unit is stated.- set a preferred altimeter unit unit:"Alexa, ask airport weather to set altimeter to inches" or"Alexa, ask airport weather to set altimeter to millibar"When in millibar mode, the altimeter reading will be prefaced by "QNH". In inches mode, no units will be stated.- set a default temperature unit:"Alexa, ask airport weather to set temperature to Fahrenheit" or"Alexa, ask airport weather to set temperature to Celsius"Real ATIS/METAR is ALWAYS in Celsius. In Fahrenheit mode, the skill will add "Fahrenheit" to the readback.- default airport: Saying the complete identifier gets tedious. You can save time with a default airport. First, use the app, specifying an airport as usual. Once it has worked, issue the following command: "Alexa, ask airport weather to set local airport."The most recent SUCCESSFULLY read station will become the default. From that point forward you can say:"Alexa, ask airport weather for the local station" or"Alexa, ask airport weather for the default station"- repetitionA real ATIS recording is a continuous loop. Alexa can mimic that with a repetition count:"Alexa, ask airport weather to set repeat to five." or"Alexa, ask airport weather to set repeat to one."(This does not affect TAF.)"Manual" at http://toolsofourtools.org/alexa-metars-and-tafsMake suggestions: dave@toolsofourtools.orgCheck out the code on github: https://github.com/djacobow/alexa_metarThis skill is not affiliated, endorsed, or supported in any way by some other thing called "MetarReader". So there you go. NOT from the good folks at "MetarReader."

Description: FeaturesWith your GPS-enabled mobile device: Now you can navigate to an exact 3 word address. For example, you can say: ‘Alexa, ask what3words to navigate to xxxx xxxx xxxx'.About what3wordswhat3words is the simplest way to talk about location. It has divided the world into 3m x 3m squares, each with a unique 3 word address. It means that you can refer to any precise location – a delivery entrance, a picnic spot or a drone landing point – using just three words. what3words is the only addressing system optimised for voice and the quickest way to enter addresses on the go. This is because when it comes to voice navigation, street addresses are frustrating for people to use and are limiting:Some addresses are difficult to distinguish between. For example, Beach Street and Beech Street sound exactly the same.Duplicate addresses cause navigation errors. Did you know there are 14 different Church Roads in London, and 632 Juarez Streets in Mexico City?There are many places that don’t have a precise address – from beaches to construction sites and specific entrances to large buildings.T&CsIf you enable this skill you accept the what3words Terms of Use at https://what3words.com/terms/ Find out about your privacy rights and the what3words Privacy and Cookies Policy at  https://what3words.com/privacy/

The Android settings vary by version and device, but you can usually get to the Do Not Disturb controls by swiping down from the top of the screen to the Quick Settings box. 2. Description: Daily Log is a full-featured dictation assistant that allows you to dictate one sentence at a time, and saves all of those sentences in a single log entry. Complete with robust reading features, Daily Log is must-have skill for anyone wanting to create notes, journal entries, Captain's logs, or just quickly capture their ideas.Our website: https://www.eklecticcore.comEnable Daily Log, and say, "Alexa, ask Daily Log for Help." A complete list of commands and other instructions are presented on the home page of the Alexa app when asking the app for Help.FEATURES THAT SET DAILY LOG APART:UNIQUE APPROACH TO DICTATION:One of the challenges with using Alexa for dictation is that she can only handle a few seconds of speech at a time before she stops to process your speech. Daily Log transforms this problem into a feature by turning Alexa into a secretary who is "taking a letter." Speak each sentence or phrase, then pause for a breath while your "secretary" writes it down. When Alexa is done writing, she'll prompt you to go on speaking. This creates a very fluid and useful dictation assistant that is not only effective, but also fun!4-DIGIT ACCESS CODES:4-digit access codes can be used to separate individual's logs, or to restrict access to your logs. And you can change your code with a simple command if necessary.- For example: "Alexa, open Daily Log and access 1234" will save logs to access code 1234, and the command "read all" will read those logs.- You can change your current access at any time by simply saying, for example, "Access 5678".- If you want to move logs from one access code to another, you can say, for example, "change access 1234 to 5678"BUILT-IN HELP:You can ask Daily Log for detailed help on various features. Try these phrases: help with account linking, help with commands, and help with reading. Built-in help is constantly being expanded, so check our website for the latest detailed help topics available: EKlecticCore.comFREE ACCOUNT LINKING TO PREVENT LOST LOGS:Sign up for a free account at EKlecticCore.com, and link it to the Daily Log skill. This will prevent your logs from being lost if you happen to disable and re-enable the Daily Log skill, or if you change your Amazon account. For more information, visit the Daily Log section on our website: EKlecticCore.comONLINE ADD, VIEW AND EDIT OF LOG ENTRIES:EKlecticCore.com offers the following premium features for those who purchase our Daily Log subscription services on our website:- Add/View/Edit Daily Log entries with web browser- Download PDF, HTML, Markdown, and Plain Text versions of Daily Log entries- Premium-level email supportFor details on more premium features in development, visit our website: EKlecticCore.com

Description: Ask Whisper Guy the time and he will tell you in a (creepy?) whisper. By default he tells you UTC/GMT time, so you would have to let him know what timezone you are in using the offset command. You only need to do this once, but can change it at any time. This is necessary as Amazon does not share timezone information with skills.Whisper Guy also comes with two additional optional features:1) Silent Open and2) Horror Quote.Silent open changes Whisper Guy's behaviour so that when you open whisper guy, there is no audio response and it waits for your input. By default this setting is off. It also removes the lead in phrase from the reply time (it is now, the time is etc.) making the response shorter. To turn 'Silent Open' on, say 'Alexa, ask Whisper Guy to set silent open to on'. To turn 'Silent Open' off, say 'Alexa, ask Whisper Guy to set silent open to off'.Horror quote changes Whisper Guy's behaviour so that five seconds after Whisper Guy tells the time, he whispers a quote from a horror movie. There are three modes for horror quote. The default mode is off. When off, Whisper Guy does not include a quote from a horror movie after he whispers the time. When on, Whisper Guy always includes a random quote from a horror movie after he whispers the time. When random, Whisper Guy randomly includes a quote (50% of the time) from a horror movie after he whispers the time. To turn horror quote on, say 'Alexa, ask Whisper Guy to set horror quote on'. To turn horror quote off, say 'Alexa, ask Whisper Guy to set horror quote off'. To change horror quote to be random, say 'Alexa, ask Whisper Guy to set horror quote random'.Below is a list of the horror quotes that Whisper Guy knows:That cold ain't the weather. Wake-Up Words 2: “Repeat after me, you are the best.”

Description: Have you always wanted to get Alexa to say certain things?  With this skill you can tell Alexa to say almost anything you can think of!  You can also hear Alexa translate your phrase from English to a different language! This skill will also allow you to select between 30+ different voices and many different voice modes to repeat your phrase! You can change Alexa's accent and have her talk like a British Male!  You can also have Alexa talk fast, slow, with a high voice, or a low voice, backwards, in reverse, whispering, in a drunk voice, as a robot, spelling out words, and saying words with random beeps!  Try it out and have tons of fun!This skill is also helpful if you want to practice your language skills.  You can translate your phrase from English to a few other different languages. After translating you can hear the translation repeated slower by saying, "say it again slow" or "say it again really slow".  You can also view the translation in your Alexa app.How it works:Tell Alexa to repeat your phrase by saying, "repeat after me".  Next, you can tell her what to say.After she tells you the phrase, you can have her repeat it by saying, "say it again".You can change the way she says the phrase by saying something like, "say it again fast".To change her accent, say something like "change accent to British male", or just say "change accent" for a random accent.After you tell her a phrase, you can also tell her to translate it to another language! (Currently max of 10 translations per week)Below are some examples on how to translate a phrase, change the accent, or change the voice mode.Translating a phrase:First start by saying "repeat after me".  Then tell Alexa your phrase.After she hears and remembers the phrase, you can say something like, "translate to German".This skill allows you to translate from English to the following languages: English, French, German, Hindi, Italian, Japanese, Portuguese, and Spanish.To hear your original phrase, you can say something like, what was my original phrase?To hear the last translated phrase, say something like, what was my last translation.You can also view the translation in your Alexa app to check for accuracy.*Note: There is currently a max of 10 translations per week.  This is subject to change.*Note: Due to the nature of voice and translation technology, translations may not always be accurate.  Always check translations before using.Changing Accents:For a list of accents, say "list accents".Just say "change accent to" followed by the name of an accent.Available Accents - Alexa, American male (1, 2), American female (1, 2, 3, 4), Australian male, Australian female, British male, British female (1, 2), French male, French female (1, 2, 3), German male, German female (1, 2), Indian female (1, 2), Italian male, Italian female (1, 2), Japanese male, Japanese female, Portuguese male, Portuguese female (1, 2), Spanish male (1, 2), Spanish female (1, 2, 3, 4, 5), boy, girlChanging Voice Modes:For a list of voice modes, say "list voice modes".Just say "say it again", followed by the name of the voice mode.Available Voice Modes - fast, slow, high, low, backwards, rewind, whisper, beep, spelled out, robot, drunk, valley girl, random*Note: Whisper mode only works for certain voices.Alexa will also include her own random comments before reciting your phrase.  To turn these off, you can say "Turn off comments".You can also turn off hint prompts, which are mentioned after your phase is repeated by saying something like, "Turn hints off".

Description: This skill allows Figure Eight (www.figure-eight.com) contributors to carry out simple voice-based crowdsourcing jobs such as topic classification or entity extraction. To be enabled to use “My daily task” skill the user needs to: (1) have a contributor account in Figure Eight, and (2) login to the platform and accept one of those tasks that require the “My daily task” skill to be performed. In the instruction section of such tasks, you will find a six-numbers code necessary to register in My Daily task. It is essential that the code you give to the skill be the correct once since it is the element that allows you to get rewarded in Figure Eight for the work you complete. The skill will also ask you for your name (feel free to use an alias in you prefer).Once registered on My Daily Task you can start carrying out tasks. The number of tasks available every day is limited, so you might be interested in activating an alarm that will remind you to perform tasks in the following days. All the tasks you perform in the days after the registration will be reward through figure eight bonuses in a few days. Before this, you can check your budget from the main menu of the task by saying “My budget”.Once registered, you land on the main skill menu, that allows you to access Skill functionalities such as the main task submission area, the budget details, and the settings. You can perform tasks of the type that was assigned to you in the Figure Eight task, such as “tweet classification”, “entities resolution”, or others. You can perform only tasks of such given type, for a maximum of five tasks per days.  You can ask for your current budget at any moment by saying “What is my budget”. The budget is calculated based on the number of tasks you have performed and will be paid to you within a few days through Figure Eight bonuses. From the main menu, you can say “help” to receive detailed information about the possible skill commands and functionality available to you.New tasks will be available through “My daily task” each day. So do not miss the opportunity to get extra rewards by coming back tomorrow. Figure Eight bonuses are waiting for you.

Description: It is estimated that tens of millions of tons of food are wasted in the United States annually.  Some of this waste may be attributed to confusion around the dates commonly printed on the foods we buy and throwing them out before necessary.  According to the Food Safety and Inspection Service of the U. S. Department of Agriculture, the Best By, Use By & Sell By dates do not refer to the safety of the food, but rather its flavor or quality.  (See details below.)   Food Manager refers to those dates as “package dates.”  Each of us can take steps to reduce the waste and give more food to the hungry.  Food Manager helps you take the first step.  It enables you to: 1. Save foods to your virtual Food Manager Pantry with package dates, specifying one of the three commonly used kinds -  Best By, Use By, or Sell By. 2. Find foods that are about to reach their package dates within any number of weeks you request so you can use or donate them, 3. Find foods that have exceeded their package dates,4. Find a specific food,5. Remove a food that has been eaten,6. Empty your Food Manager Pantry of foods that are past their package dates, and7. Get a list of all the foods in your Food Manager Pantry.These 7 features of Food Manager will enable you to more efficiently manage your foods so that you need to throw out less food and can donate more.  With a little effort, it’s a win-win!See our demo video on YouTube at:https://youtu.be/rGUZLiPpD0s From the Food Safety and Inspection Service of the United States Department of Agriculture (www.fsis.usda.gov):•	A "Best if Used By/Before" indicates when a product will be of best flavor or quality. It is not a purchase or safety date. •	A "Sell-By" date tells the store how long to display the product for sale for inventory management. It is not a safety date. •	A "Use-By" date is the last date recommended for the use of the product while at peak quality. It is not a safety date except for when used on infant formula.

Description: Simply put, the Unix timestamp is a way to track time as a running total of seconds. This count starts at the Unix Epoch on January 1st, 1970 at UTC. Therefore, the Unix timestamp is merely the number of seconds between a particular date and the Unix Epoch. It should also be pointed out that this point in time technically does not change no matter where you are located on the globe. This is very useful to computer systems for tracking and sorting dated information in dynamic and distributed applications both online and client side. The reason why Unix timestamps are used by many webmasters is because they can represent all time zones at once.

Using some libs to get timezone for the city, and then get the local time of it;2. Using OpenWeatherMap API for acquiring weather data for a certain city.## Challenges I ran intoIncorperating different sources of information together.## Accomplishments that I'm proud ofCombinatorial is a simple ideology. But combining two highly combined can really make a change.## What's next for TimeAndWeatherMore active form of displaying results. Support for more forms of input. Also, currently it only supports cities in US.
